source: Invest Like the Best — AI, semiconductors, and the robotic frontier (EP.385)
summary: Synthetic data solves the training data scarcity problem, extending the runway for scaling laws
summary_extended: Baker argues that the 'data wall' bear case — the concern that AI scaling laws would stall due to exhaustion of high-quality human-generated training data — has been empirically refuted by the emergence of synthetic data techniques. He cites two specific technical papers as evidence: Anthropic's Claude 3.5 technical paper and NVIDIA's Nemotron technical paper, both of which demonstrate that models trained on synthetic data continue to improve, even though the underlying mechanism remains poorly understood. Kevin Scott, Microsoft's CTO, has reportedly seen early GPT-5 checkpoints and confirmed that scaling laws are continuing to hold, providing additional corroboration from an insider vantage point. The investment implication is that the runway for frontier model scaling remains intact, which is broadly bullish for companies investing heavily in compute infrastructure and frontier model development, though Baker expresses uncertainty about whether synthetic data will continue working indefinitely.
detail: Baker believes the data wall bear case from ~9 months prior has been addressed, citing the Claude 3.5 technical paper and NVIDIA's Nemotron technical paper as evidence that synthetic data works. He acknowledges no one understands why it works, but empirically it appears to. Kevin Scott (Microsoft CTO) has seen early GPT-5 checkpoints and confirmed scaling laws are continuing.
confidence_evidence: Baker qualifies with 'no one understands why' and 'will it continue working? I don't know'; cites specific technical papers as evidence
quote_fragment: it does look like synthetic data works. No one understands why

ticker_audit.reasoning: If scaling laws continue and synthetic data extends the training data runway, demand for GPU compute used in large-scale model training remains robust, directly benefiting NVDA as the dominant supplier of AI training hardware.

MSFT is mentioned only because its CTO Kevin Scott has seen early GPT-5 checkpoints. This is used as anecdotal corroboration of scaling law continuation, not as an investment thesis about MSFT itself. MSFT is neither the subject of nor a direct beneficiary uniquely implied by this specific thesis.
